h_1.text:
The north spine is a vertical column of infrastructure — pipes, cables, ventilation ducts — that runs the full height of the station. Maintenance shafts branch off it like ribs. Most of them are unlit, and the ones that descend toward the lower decks have been officially condemned for years.

You find shaft seven easily enough. The access hatch is wedged open with a pry bar — other scavengers have been here before you. Beyond the hatch, a ladder descends into darkness. Your headlamp cuts a thin beam through the dusty air.

As you prepare to descend, you notice boot prints in the dust. Fresh ones, leading to a side corridor.

choices.text: Follow the boot prints
Investigate the side corridor
Ignore them and descend the shaft immediately

h_2.text:
You find him in a dead-end service corridor near shaft seven, exactly where Zara said. He has made a nest of sorts — a sleeping bag, a portable heater, MRE wrappers. He is younger than you expected. Short military haircut growing out. Haunted eyes.

He has a weapon drawn before you finish rounding the corner, but he does not fire.

'I'm not Imperial anymore,' he says quickly. 'If Malachar sent you—'

'He didn't,' you say. 'Zara told me about you.'

Something loosens in his face. He lowers the weapon — a service pistol, standard Imperial issue. 'Zara. Right.' He exhales. 'I'm Voss. Corporal Voss, formerly of the Inquisitorial Guard. I walked away three weeks ago when Malachar ordered the execution of a Qeth archaeological team. Civilians. Scientists. He killed them for a map.'

His jaw tightens. 'The same map that's on your data chip, I'm guessing. He's coming here for the Void Compass. And he will burn this station to the hull if he has to.' Voss stands. 'I know his playbook. His tactics. His access codes. Take me with you and I can get us through doors that would otherwise stay shut.'

choices.text: Accept Voss as an ally
Decline — you work alone

h_3.text:
'Good,' Voss says, and there is visible relief in his face. He packs up his meagre camp in thirty seconds flat — military efficiency. 'The lower decks are dangerous. Atmosphere is thin in places, and the radiation shielding failed in some sections years ago. But the biggest problem is the wildlife.'

He checks his pistol. 'The maintenance levels between here and the sealed decks have duct crawlers — mutated vermin, mostly. Nasty but manageable. Below that, in the sealed sections...' He hesitates. 'Malachar's last report mentioned something else down there. Something bigger. His scouts didn't come back.'

Voss clips a small device to his belt — a signal scrambler. 'This will keep Imperial sensors from tracking us. For a while, anyway. Let's move.'

choices.text: Descend into the maintenance shafts together

h_4.text:
Voss nods slowly. 'Your call. But if you run into Malachar down there...' He does not finish the sentence. He does not need to.

You leave him in his corridor and continue toward the maintenance shafts. The station groans around you — the sound of metal expanding and contracting in the void. Somewhere far below, something is waiting.

choices.text: Continue into the maintenance shafts

h_new_freq.text:
Voss leads the way toward shaft seven, moving with the careful economy of someone trained to clear corridors. He checks each junction before waving you forward. Old habits.

As you descend the first ladder together, he pauses on the rungs below you and pulls a scrap of metal foil from his kit. He scratches seven digits into it with his thumbnail and hands it up.

'Comm frequency,' he says. 'Short burst, encrypted, hard to triangulate. Old Imperial field protocol. If we get separated — and down here, we might — use it. Point-to-point only.' He meets your eyes. 'Don't broadcast it. There are things listening on this station that aren't human and aren't crawlers.'

choices.text: Pocket the foil and continue the descent

h_shaft_1.text:
The shaft drops through three levels of dead infrastructure. Each level is darker than the last. The ladder rungs are slick with condensation, and in places the metal is pitted with corrosion — your hands come away gritty with rust.

At the second level landing, a junction platform branches in two directions. To the left, a narrow access tunnel slopes downward at a steep angle — the main descent route, marked with faded Station Authority chevrons. To the right, a wider service passage runs horizontally, its ceiling strung with dead cable runs. A faint draught comes from the right — air moving, which means it connects to something open below.

From the left tunnel, you hear the distant drip of liquid. From the right, nothing at all.

choices.text: Take the main descent route to the left
Explore the service passage to the right

h_shaft_2.text:
The tunnel angles down steeply — forty-five degrees at least. You brace yourself against the walls and half-walk, half-slide. The dripping grows louder.

You emerge onto a landing that is ankle-deep in coolant fluid. The stuff is cold and viscous, a faintly luminescent green that casts sick light across the walls. A ruptured pipe above has been leaking for years, filling this section of the shaft like a cistern. Debris floats in the fluid — insulation fragments, cable sheathing, something that might be fabric.

Across the flooded landing, the shaft continues downward. The only way forward is through.

choices.text: Wade through the coolant
Use the repair kit to seal the pipe and find a bypass hatch

h_shaft_4.text:
You step into the coolant. It is cold enough to make your teeth ache — industrial refrigerant, not designed for human contact. The fluid comes up past your ankles, soaking through your boots. Each step sends ripples across the surface, disturbing the floating debris.

Halfway across, your foot catches on something submerged — a body. You don't look down. You keep moving.

The far side of the landing is dry. You haul yourself up onto the grating and shake the worst of the coolant from your legs. Your feet are numb. The shaft continues down.

choices.text: Continue the descent

h_shaft_4b.text:
You pull out the repair kit and examine the ruptured pipe. The break is clean — metal fatigue, not impact damage. You apply sealant paste to the worst of the leak, reducing the flow to a trickle. It won't hold forever, but it buys you time.

With the flow reduced, you spot what you were hoping for — a bypass hatch in the wall, half-submerged but accessible now. You pry it open with your vibro-knife. Beyond, a narrow maintenance crawl runs parallel to the flooded section, dry and passable.

You squeeze through and emerge on the far side of the landing, boots dry, coolant avoided.

choices.text: Continue the descent

h_shaft_3.text:
The service passage runs straight for fifty metres before opening into a small equipment bay. Lockers line the walls — all forced open, all empty. Other scavengers have been thorough.

But in the far corner, slumped against a locker, you find something they left behind. A body. Not recent — the station's dry recycled air has turned it into something between a corpse and a mummy. A scavenger, judging by the gear harness. The harness is stripped clean, but there is a personal log chip still slotted into the dead woman's wrist unit.

You pull the chip and slot it into your own unit. A voice, thin and distorted by time: 'Day fourteen. Deeper than anyone I know has gone. Past the coolant breach, past the crawler nests. The coordinates are good — the Qeth data matches the old station blueprints. There's a sealed research section below Cargo Bay 12. That's where it is.'

A pause. Static. Then: 'Day sixteen. Something else is down here. Not the crawlers. Something organised. I found tool marks on a door — fresh ones. Someone or something is opening hatches that have been sealed for decades. I'm going to—'

The recording ends.

choices.text: Take the passage back and continue the descent
Search the equipment bay more thoroughly

h_shaft_3b.text:
You search the bay methodically. The lockers are cleaned out, but behind the body you find a ventilation grille that has been pulled loose. Beyond it, a vertical drop — a secondary shaft that bypasses the next two levels entirely.

The dead scavenger found this shortcut. She just never made it back to use it.

The drop is manageable — four metres, with cable runs for handholds. It would skip a significant section of the descent.

choices.text: Take the shortcut down the secondary shaft
Go back to the main route — shortcuts in the dark make you nervous

h_shaft_5.text:
The shaft levels out briefly at what the station schematics would call Sublevel Nine. The air here has a different quality — thinner, metallic, with an undertone of something biological. Mould or fungal growth coats the walls in irregular patches, dark and faintly damp.

Your headlamp catches movement ahead — a pale shape, fast, scuttling along the ceiling of the shaft. A duct crawler, smaller than the ones you've heard about but still the size of a terrier. It freezes in your light, mandibles twitching, then bolts deeper into the shaft.

You hear more of them ahead. Clicking. Rustling. A nest, maybe. The shaft continues straight through their territory. To the right, a sealed ventilation iris offers a possible alternate route — a circular mechanical valve sized for cargo airflow, frozen solid with years of corrosion.

choices.text: Push through the crawler territory
Try to cut through the ventilation iris with your vibro-knife

h_shaft_6.text:
You move fast and loud — the scavenger's trick for crawlers. They are territorial but cowardly in small numbers. Most of them scatter as you push through, pale bodies flowing up walls and into crevices like water running in reverse.

One does not scatter. A larger specimen — mother of the nest, maybe — drops from the ceiling and lands on the shaft floor in front of you, legs splayed wide, mandibles open. It hisses. Behind you, the smaller crawlers are regrouping, emboldened by her stand.

h_new_vibro_knife.text:
You draw your vibro-knife and thumb the oscillator to maximum. The blade hums — a high, thin whine that you feel in your teeth more than hear. The ventilation iris is a circular hatch of interlocking metal leaves, designed to regulate airflow between shaft levels. Years of corrosion have fused the locking ring solid.

You set the blade against the oxidised ring and cut. The vibro-knife earns its keep — the molecular edge bites into corroded metal that would defeat any conventional tool. Sparks shower in the dark. Two minutes of steady pressure, and the ring shears through. The iris leaves sag inward, opening a gap wide enough to squeeze through.

Beyond, a clean shaft drops to the next level — bypassing the crawler nest entirely. Your arms ache from the sustained cutting, but the route is clear.

choices.text: Squeeze through and continue the descent

h_shaft_7.text:
The shaft opens into a broader corridor — a transitional space between the upper maintenance levels and the deep infrastructure below. The walls here are older. The station was built in layers over decades, and this deep you can see the original construction: heavier alloys, cruder welds, design philosophy from a different era.

Ahead, the corridor splits. The left branch slopes downward at a sharp angle — the direct route deeper, but the ceiling is buckled. Something structural gave way at some point, and the passage narrows to a tight squeeze between collapsed deck plates. The right branch is wider and clear, but curves away from your bearing — a longer route that arcs around whatever caused the collapse.

choices.text: Take the collapsed left branch — tight but direct
Take the clear right branch — longer but safer

h_shaft_8a.text:
You drop to your belly and crawl. The collapsed deck plates press close — close enough that you can feel the cold metal against your back as you wriggle forward. Your headlamp illuminates nothing but the next half-metre of corroded floor.

Halfway through, a groan. The metal shifts above you — settling, adjusting to your weight. You freeze. The groan fades. You keep crawling.

The squeeze lasts fifteen metres. It feels like fifteen kilometres. When you emerge on the other side, your hands are shaking and your elbows are raw. But you're through, and the shaft ahead is clear.

choices.text: Continue deeper

h_shaft_8b.text:
The right branch curves in a wide arc around the collapse zone. It is an older section — the walls are bare metal here, unfinished, with exposed rivet lines and conduit bundles that were never given proper housing. The station's bones.

The detour takes twenty minutes. You pass through two empty junction rooms and a decommissioned pump station, its machinery long since stripped for parts. The only sound is your own breathing and the distant, ever-present groan of the station's hull.

choices.text: Continue deeper

h_shaft_9.text:
You are deep now. Deep enough that the electromagnetic interference is constant — your headlamp flickers in stuttering pulses, and the data chip in your pocket gives off a faint warmth, as if its circuits are fighting the static.

The shaft descends one more level and opens onto a wider passage. This is the boundary — below here are the condemned sections, the sealed decks where the atmosphere recyclers failed and the Station Authority stopped sending repair crews. Everything below this point is supposed to be dead.

But the air moves. And it carries a smell — old, biological, rank. Something down here is very much alive.

choices.text: Descend into the condemned sections

h_shaft_10.text:
The passage widens as you descend the final ladder. At the bottom, a horizontal crawlway stretches in both directions. To the left, a side corridor branches off into shadow — and from it, you hear voices. Not Imperial. Rough, station dialect. The kind of voices that belong to people who work for Harko.

To the right, the crawlway continues toward the main junction that leads to the deeper service corridors and cargo bays.

choices.text: Those voices — someone is in trouble in that corridor
Investigate the voices
Continue to the main junction

h_shaft_10b.text:
You edge down the side corridor and peer around a junction. Two of Harko's enforcers — you recognise the type, heavyset men in patched coveralls with shock-batons on their belts — are shaking down a lone figure. A squatter, probably. Someone who wandered into territory Harko considers his.

The enforcers are focused on their target. They haven't noticed you. This isn't your fight and it isn't worth the risk. You pull back and return to the main crawlway.

choices.text: Continue to the main junction

h_new_second_chance.text:
You edge down the side corridor and the voices become clear. Two of Harko's enforcers — big men in patched coveralls, shock-batons drawn — have someone backed against the wall. It's Voss. He has his pistol out but held low, not aiming. He knows better than to start a firefight in Harko's tunnels.

'Squatters pay the toll,' one of the enforcers says. 'Or squatters lose teeth. Your choice, soldier boy.'

Voss sees you over their shoulders. His expression doesn't change — he's disciplined enough for that — but something shifts in his eyes. Recognition. A question.

You have a decision to make.

choices.text: Step forward and back Voss up
Tell them Voss is with you — under Harko's arrangement
Walk past — his problem, not yours

h_new_second_chance_b.text:
You step into the corridor with your hand on your vibro-knife and your headlamp full in their faces. 'He's with me.'

The enforcers squint against the light. They assess you — the knife, the scavenger's gear harness, the look in your eyes. The look of someone who has done this calculation before and come out ahead.

'Two of you, two of us,' one says. But his heart isn't in it. Harko doesn't pay enough for this kind of odds.

They leave. Slowly, with muttered threats, but they leave.

Voss holsters his pistol. 'I owe you one.' He is quiet for a moment. 'I was wrong to let you walk away. Take me with you. I know what's down there — I know Malachar's codes, his patrol patterns, his blind spots. You need me.'

He pulls a scrap of metal foil from his kit, scratches seven digits into it, and hands it to you. 'Comm frequency. Old Imperial field protocol. Point-to-point only. Don't broadcast it.'

You pocket the foil.

choices.text: Continue to the main junction together

h_new_second_chance_c.text:
'He's with me,' you say, stepping into the light. 'And I'm with Harko. Ask him if you want — name's on the list.'

The enforcers exchange a look. One of them pulls a comm unit and mutters into it. A long pause. Then a crackle of response. The enforcer's expression sours, but he clips the baton back to his belt.

'Harko says you're clear.' He jerks his head at Voss. 'Both of you. But next time, tell your friends to check in at the gate.'

They disappear down the corridor.

Voss stares at you. 'You know Harko?' He shakes his head. 'I don't want to know. But I owe you one.' He steps closer. 'Let me come with you. I know Malachar's codes, his patterns. You'll need me before this is over.'

He hands you a scrap of foil with seven digits scratched into it. 'Comm frequency. Point-to-point, encrypted. Just in case.'

choices.text: Continue to the main junction together

h_5.text:
The maintenance shafts narrow as you descend. The walls are streaked with condensation and something that might be mould or might be worse. Your headlamp flickers — the electromagnetic interference this deep in the station plays havoc with unshielded electronics.

Three levels down, the shaft opens into a horizontal crawlway. The air is thinner here and tastes of metal. Your data chip's coordinates point further down, but the crawlway branches — left toward what the station schematics call 'Service Corridor 7-Alpha,' and right toward 'Cargo Bay 12.'

A sound echoes from the left branch. Scratching. Skittering. Something moving fast across metal.

choices.text: Go left toward Service Corridor 7-Alpha
Go right toward Cargo Bay 12

h_6.text:
The crawlway opens into Service Corridor 7-Alpha — a long, low-ceilinged passage lined with pipe runs and junction boxes. Emergency lighting flickers in intermittent red pulses, casting everything in a hellish strobe.

The skittering is louder here. You see movement — a shape the size of a large dog, pale and segmented, scuttling along the ceiling with too many legs. A DUCT CRAWLER. The station's lower decks breed them — mutated vermin that feed on cable insulation and anything else organic enough to digest.

It drops from the ceiling toward you, mandibles clicking.

h_7.text:
The crawler twitches once and goes still, its pale ichor pooling on the deck plates. You step over it and continue along the corridor.

Ahead, the passage ends at a heavy bulkhead door — one of the Station Authority seals that locks off the lower decks. A red light blinks on the access panel beside it. RESTRICTED — AUTHORISED PERSONNEL ONLY.

The data chip's coordinates lie beyond this door.

choices.text: Use the security keycard
Use the repair kit to bypass the lock
Ask Voss to use his Imperial codes
Try to force the door

h_8.text:
You throw your weight against the bulkhead door. It does not move. You try your vibro-knife on the lock mechanism, but the housing is reinforced tritanium — the blade skitters across the surface without leaving a mark.

This door was built to withstand explosive decompression. It is not going to yield to brute force. You need a keycard, a bypass tool, or someone with the right codes.

choices.text: Use the security keycard
Use the repair kit to bypass the lock
Ask Voss to use his Imperial codes
Go back and try Cargo Bay 12 instead
Push deeper into the shafts — there must be another entrance

h_9.text:
Cargo Bay 12 is cavernous — a vast dark space that swallows your headlamp beam. Shipping containers stand in rusting rows like the buildings of a dead city. The air is cold here, noticeably colder than the corridors above.

You pick your way between the containers, following the data chip's coordinates. The bay connects to the lower decks through a freight loading tunnel at its far end. But as you move deeper into the bay, you notice something wrong. The containers nearest the loading tunnel have been torn open — not by tools, but by something with terrible strength. Metal peeled back like paper. Claw marks gouged into steel.

And there is a smell. Biological. Rank. The smell of something large and alive.

choices.text: Press on toward the loading tunnel
Search the torn containers for salvage
Check the wall terminal
Go back and try Service Corridor 7-Alpha

h_10.text:
You are halfway to the loading tunnel when it emerges from behind a container stack. Huge. Three metres tall, hunched, its body a mass of grey-green muscle and chitinous plates. It might have been human once — the basic shape is there — but radiation and decades of isolation in the station's dead zones have turned it into something else entirely.

A MUTANT STALKER. Its eyes are milky white and sightless, but its head tracks you with terrible precision. It hunts by sound and smell. It opens a mouth full of needle teeth and screams — a sound like tearing metal.

It charges.

h_11.text:
You check the torn containers cautiously. Most are empty — whatever cargo they held was consumed or destroyed long ago. But in one, wedged behind a collapsed shelf unit, you find a repair kit in a sealed case and, wedged beside it, a military stim-pack — stimulant and analgesic, enough to keep a wounded soldier fighting. Both still sealed.

You also find bones. Human bones, cracked and gnawed. Whatever tore these containers open, it has been feeding. You pocket the repair kit and move on quickly — but the find settles something in you. You know this kind of dark. You know how to work it.

choices.text: Press on toward the loading tunnel
Go back and try Service Corridor 7-Alpha

h_new_terminal.text:
Near the bay's east wall, half-hidden behind a toppled shelving unit, a terminal screen flickers. Still powered — drawing from a circuit the rest of the bay has long since abandoned. You brush dust from the screen and it responds to your touch, sluggishly cycling through a login sequence before defaulting to the last user's session.

Station Authority records. Duty logs from Warrant Officer Ekta Sain, assigned to Lower Deck Research Inspections. The entries are dated five years ago.

The first dozen are routine. Atmosphere readings, seal integrity checks, radiation monitoring. Methodical. Professional. Then the tone changes.

'14-Nov: The artefact in Lab 7 is not inert. Instrumentation shows low-level EM emissions on a non-standard frequency. Crystalline structure appears to have internal luminescence not attributable to ambient radiation. Recommend immediate lockdown and notification to the Bureau of Antiquities.'

'17-Nov: Bureau notification sent via priority channel.'

'20-Nov: Bureau response received: No action required. Continue normal operations. Do not discuss Lab 7 contents with non-cleared personnel.'

There are no further entries. Warrant Officer Sain's log simply stops. Two months later, the Imperial garrison withdrew from New Babylon Station entirely.

Someone at the Bureau already knew. They told the garrison to leave it alone. And then they pulled everyone out.

choices.text: Return to Cargo Bay 12

h_12.text:
You kneel beside the access panel and pry off the housing with your vibro-knife. Inside, the lock mechanism is standard Station Authority hardware — old but well-engineered. You pull out your repair kit and get to work, bridging circuits and rerouting the authorisation pathway.

It takes five tense minutes. Twice you short a connection and have to start over. But finally, the panel chimes green and the bulkhead door grinds open, revealing a dark passage beyond.

You are through.

choices.text: Enter the lower decks

h_13.text:
Voss steps up to the access panel and plugs in a small device from his belt. 'Imperial override codes,' he says, fingers moving quickly. 'Malachar changes them every rotation, but the Station Authority systems haven't been updated in years. They'll still accept the old ones.'

The panel flashes amber, then green. The bulkhead door groans open.

Voss grins — the first time you have seen him smile. 'Being a traitor has its perks.'

choices.text: Enter the lower decks

h_14.text:
You press the security keycard against the reader. The panel flashes green immediately and the bulkhead door slides open with a deep hydraulic sigh. Beyond it, the air is noticeably colder. The emergency lighting here is blue instead of red — the lower decks operate on a different power grid.

You step through. The door seals behind you with a sound of finality.

choices.text: Continue into the lower decks

h_new_stalker_win.text:
The stalker crashes to the deck plates, its massive body convulsing once before going still. The silence that follows is absolute — even the background hum of the station seems muted, as if the bay itself is holding its breath.

You step over the body. Up close, the thing is even worse — the remnants of human anatomy visible beneath the chitinous plates. A jaw. Fingers, fused and overgrown. Whatever this was, it lived down here for a very long time.

The freight loading tunnel at the far end of Cargo Bay 12 slopes steeply downward — a ramp designed for cargo haulers, wide enough for two abreast. At the base of the ramp, another bulkhead door. This one has already been dealt with: something tore the door half off its track years ago, warping the frame and leaving a gap wide enough to squeeze through. The stalker's work, probably — the claw marks match.

You turn sideways and push through the gap. The metal groans but holds.

Beyond, the lower decks stretch out in cold silence. The air is thin and tastes of ozone. The emergency lighting is blue here — a different power grid, a different era of construction. Everything feels older. Heavier. Built to last by people who expected it to matter.

The data chip pulses warm in your pocket. You are close.

choices.text: Continue into the lower decks
